title: Database cluster complete outage
description: Datadog database monitoring detected complete unavailability of the primary PostgreSQL cluster (postgres-cluster, 3-node RDS Multi-AZ) at 22:17 UTC on Oct 30. All 3 replica-nodes had failed over simultaneously after the primary instance ran out of available connections (max_connections=500 exceeded; actual connection count hit 502 at failure). The connection surge was traced to a batch processing job (nightly-analytics-etl) that was mis-configured with a connection pool size of 300 per worker, and had been scaled to 2 workers by an auto-scaling policy triggered by CPU load — resulting in 600 attempted connections against a cluster with a 500 connection limit. All application services that depended on the database began returning HTTP 500 at 22:17 UTC.
detection_method: Datadog RDS Integration monitor — 'PostgreSQL connection count > 95% of max_connections' threshold trigger; simultaneous HTTP 500 spike on all application services

timeline.event: Datadog RDS monitor alerted: postgres-cluster connection count reached 502/500 (max_connections exceeded); primary instance unavailable; RDS Multi-AZ failover initiated automatically but replica also overwhelmed; all app services returning HTTP 500; PagerDuty P1 fired

On-call DBA r.fontaine@company.com acknowledged; nightly-analytics-etl job identified as connection surge source (RDS performance insights showing 2 ETL workers × 300 pool size = 600 attempted connections); ETL job immediately terminated via ECS task stop command

Root cause confirmed: nightly-analytics-etl connection pool size was 300 per worker (configured Oct 22 by a.chen@company.com for performance optimization); autoscaling policy scaled to 2 workers due to Oct 30 end-of-month analytics load spike; combined effect (600 connections) exceeded max_connections limit

ETL job terminated; database connections drained and cluster recovered; pgBouncer connection pooling deployed as a proxy layer; ETL job pool size reconfigured to 50 per worker with max_workers=4 cap; deployed PgBouncer with pool_size=200 as database-level connection management layer
All application services confirmed healthy; Datadog monitoring green; ETL job re-run completed successfully through PgBouncer; max_connections RDS alarm threshold corrected; incident closed

resolution_summary: Terminating the nightly-analytics-etl job freed database connections within 15 minutes, allowing the cluster to recover without a full RDS multi-AZ failover. PgBouncer was deployed as a connection pooling proxy limiting total database connections to 200 regardless of application-layer pool configurations. The ETL job pool size was reduced from 300 to 50 per worker, with a hard cap of 4 maximum workers, ensuring the maximum possible connection demand is 200 — safely within the RDS max_connections limit of 500. Total outage duration was 5.5 hours due to the time required to deploy and validate PgBouncer.
root_cause_analysis: A performance optimization change on Oct 22 increased the nightly-analytics-etl connection pool size from 50 to 300 per worker to reduce query latency. This change was tested against a staging RDS instance with max_connections=1000 (a larger instance type than production) and passed without issue. The production RDS instance has max_connections=500. The ETL auto-scaling policy was configured to scale up based on CPU utilization, not connection pool usage; the Oct 30 end-of-month analytics load spike triggered scale-out from 1 to 2 workers (automated, no human approval required). Two workers × 300 pool size = 600 attempted connections, exceeding the 500 hard limit. No alerting was configured for the 'connection pool size of ETL service' metric, so the risk was invisible until the cluster failed.
business_impact: Complete database unavailability for 5.5 hours; all application services returning HTTP 500; $391K estimated revenue and productivity impact
remediation_actions: Terminated nightly-analytics-etl ECS tasks: aws ecs stop-task --cluster analytics --task <task-id> for both running workers; connection count dropped from 502 to 34 (legitimate application connections) within 3 minutes
Confirmed RDS cluster recovery: SHOW max_connections; returned 500; SELECT count(*) FROM pg_stat_activity; returned 34; primary instance accepting new connections; all 3 replica-nodes synchronized

Deployed PgBouncer connection pooler on 2 EC2 instances in the prod VPC: configured pool_size=200, default_pool_size=20 per database/user pair; all application services DNS updated to point to PgBouncer endpoint

Reconfigured nightly-analytics-etl: connection pool size reduced from 300 to 50 per worker; max ECS service desired_count capped at 4 (max 200 connections, well within PgBouncer's 200-cap); deployed and validated

Ran ETL job test with 4 workers via PgBouncer: SELECT count(*) FROM pg_stat_activity peaked at 187 — confirmed within safe limits; full nightly job re-run completed in 3.2 hours (vs normal 2.8 hours — acceptable trade-off)
Reviewed all 14 microservices for connection pool sizes: found 3 services with pool sizes >50; reduced to 20 each to stay within PgBouncer queue budget
preventive_measures: PgBouncer connection pooler deployed permanently as a mandatory intermediary: no application can now exceed database-level connection limits regardless of application pool configuration
max_connections alarm set at 70% threshold (350/500): triggers PagerDuty P2 warning, giving 30 minutes to investigate before reaching the 500-connection failure point
ETL job autoscaling policy updated: scale-out now gated on both CPU >70% AND available PostgreSQL connection headroom >100 (via a custom CloudWatch metric from PgBouncer stats)
Database connection configuration review added to pre-deployment checklist: any change to connection pool sizes requires a staged test against an RDS instance of the same instance class as production
security_controls_updated: Datadog RDS integration alarms updated: 'PostgreSQL connections > 350 (70% of max)' now triggers P2 alert with 'connection approaching limit' context; the previous threshold was 95% (475/500) — too close to failure
PgBouncer deploy: database-level hard connection limit now enforced at PgBouncer layer regardless of application config; max_client_conn=1000, pool_size=200

AWS RDS parameter group updated: max_connections for postgres-cluster increased to 800 as a graduated buffer (RDS db.r6g.2xlarge can support 800); provides headroom while PgBouncer architecture is validated
ECS service autoscaling policies for analytics jobs updated to include a custom CloudWatch metric: 'DBConnectionHeadroom' must be >100 before any scale-out event is approved
documentation_updated: Database Operations Runbook updated: PostgreSQL connection exhaustion response procedure with PgBouncer stats commands and ETL termination procedure
ETL Engineering Standards updated: mandatory connection pool documentation in service config; autoscaling policies must account for max connection contribution
PgBouncer Architecture Document created: deployment topology, pool configuration, monitoring setup, and failover procedure
Incident postmortem published: OPS-2025-1031 with timeline, $391K impact calculation, and 4 corrective actions with owners and deadlines
lessons_learned: Connection pool sizes are infrastructure-level configuration with service-wide blast radius when misconfigured — a change to a single batch job's pool size caused a complete database outage affecting all services. Connection pooling infrastructure (PgBouncer/pgpool) provides the correct architectural isolation: applications believe they have unlimited connection capability but the pooler enforces database-level limits transparently. Autoscaling policies must account for the resource cost of each additional worker, not just CPU/memory — database connections are a scalable resource that autoscaling policies were completely blind to.
follow_up_tasks: DB-OPS-001: Deploy PgBouncer in HA configuration (2 active instances with health check-based failover) — Owner: DBA, Due: 2025-11-14
DB-OPS-002: Review and adjust connection pool sizes for all 14 microservices to stay within PgBouncer budget — Owner: Platform, Due: 2025-11-07
DB-OPS-003: Implement 'DBConnectionHeadroom' custom CloudWatch metric for all ETL autoscaling policies — Owner: Platform, Due: 2025-11-07
DB-OPS-004: Upgrade postgres-cluster to db.r6g.4xlarge to increase max_connections to 1600 and reduce future connection constraint risk — Owner: DBA, Due: 2025-11-21
resolution: ETL job terminated; PgBouncer deployed as connection pooling layer; all application services restored; max_connections alarm threshold corrected to 70%
action_taken: Resolved by infrastructure-team within 470 minutes; 5.5-hour outage due to connection exhaustion cascade; PgBouncer architecture deployed as permanent fix
detection_gap: No monitoring existed on application-level connection pool sizes or aggregate connection demand across autoscaled ETL workers; autoscaling policy evaluated CPU utilization only and was completely blind to the database connection cost of each additional worker; no alerting threshold was configured for connection count approaching the RDS max_connections limit until it was breached
what_went_well: Terminating the nightly-analytics-etl ECS tasks freed database connections within 3 minutes, allowing the cluster to recover without a full RDS Multi-AZ recreate
RDS Performance Insights correctly identified the ETL job as the connection surge source within 8 minutes of investigation
All application services were stateless and reconnected automatically once connection headroom was restored
what_went_wrong: ETL connection pool size was increased from 50 to 300 per worker without testing against a production-equivalent RDS instance (staging used db.r6g.4xlarge vs production db.r6g.2xlarge)
Autoscaling policy scaled ETL workers based solely on CPU utilization with no awareness of database connection demand
No alerting existed for connection count approaching max_connections until the hard limit of 500 was breached and the cluster became unavailable
